See Tompkins v. Cyr, 202 F.3d 770, 787 (5th Cir. 2000) (evidence must be relevant to a "consequential fact" in the case at bar); Collins v. Wayne Corp., 621 F.2d 777, 784 (5th Cir. 1980) (excluding evidence that had no bearing on the specific issues before the jury).

The cited parenthetical has no basis in this opinion.

proposition: Rule 403 grants trial courts wide discretion to exclude evidence where the risk of prejudice outweighs probative value.
cited_case: United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979)
retrieved_case: United States v. Stephen Roderick McRae
brief_sentence: The Fifth Circuit has emphasized that Rule 403 grants trial courts "wide discretion" to exclude evidence where the risk of prejudice outweighs probative value. United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979).
quote_check: [{"quote": "wide discretion", "result": "VERBATIM", "similarity": 1.0, "matched_passage": ". As to rulings of this sort, it is a commonplace that the trial court enjoys a wide discretion, one which we do not disturb except for abuse."}]
brief_block: The Fifth Circuit has emphasized that Rule 403 grants trial courts "wide discretion" to exclude evidence where the risk of prejudice outweighs probative value. United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979).
opinion_block: The remaining two claimed errors involve evidentiary rulings by the district court. As to rulings of this sort, it is a commonplace that the trial court enjoys a wide discretion, one which we do not disturb except for abuse.
finding_analysis:
The quoted phrase "wide discretion" does appear verbatim on page 707 of the McRae opinion, but the brief's framing misattributes its scope. McRae does not say that Rule 403 itself grants trial courts "wide discretion"; rather, the opinion uses that phrase as a general observation about evidentiary rulings by district courts ("the trial court enjoys a wide discretion, one which we do not disturb except for abuse"). The opinion then discusses Rule 403 in the specific context of challenged photographs of the murder victim.

McRae does support the broader proposition that trial courts have wide discretion over Rule 403 determinations -- the opinion describes Rule 403's function, noting that "it is only unfair prejudice, substantially outweighing probative value, which permits exclusion of relevant matter under Rule 403" and that application of Rule 403 "must be cautious and sparing." The opinion's characterization of the trial court's discretion supports the brief's general proposition, though the direct quote "wide discretion" is a generic statement about evidentiary rulings rather than a Rule-403-specific holding. The minor stretch in attributing the phrase specifically to Rule 403 is a light rewording rather than a material misrepresentation of the case.

The Supreme Court, quoting the Advisory Committee's Notes to Rule 403, explained that "'Unfair prejudice' within its context means an undue tendency to suggest decision on an improper basis, commonly, though not necessarily, an emotional one."

The brief accurately captures the substance of this passage, which is a canonical articulation of what "unfair prejudice" means under Rule 403. The citation is used appropriately and faithfully in support of the proposition offered.

proposition: The Collateral Source Rule is of common law origin and bars a tortfeasor from reducing liability because the plaintiff received compensation from an independent source.
brief_sentence: The Collateral Source Rule is of "common law origin" and bars a tortfeasor from reducing their liability because the plaintiff received compensation from an independent source. Bozeman v. State, 879 So. 2d 692, 698 (La. 2004).

brief_block: The Collateral Source Rule is of "common law origin" and bars a tortfeasor from reducing their liability because the plaintiff received compensation from an independent source. Bozeman v. State, 879 So. 2d 692, 698 (La. 2004).
opinion_block: The collateral source rule is a rule of evidence and damages that is of common law origin, yet embraced and applied by Louisiana courts. . . . Under the collateral source rule, a tortfeasor may not benefit, and an injured plaintiff's tort recovery may not be reduced, because of monies received by the plaintiff from sources independent of the tortfeasor's procuration or contribution.
finding_analysis: The Bozeman court expressly describes the collateral source rule as being 'of common law origin' (the quoted phrase appears verbatim) and articulates the rule in the same terms used by the brief: a tortfeasor cannot benefit, and a plaintiff's recovery cannot be reduced, because of payments from sources independent of the tortfeasor. The brief's characterization accurately tracks the opinion.

proposition: Spoliation sanctions in the Fifth Circuit require proof that the party acted in bad faith; mere negligence is insufficient.
cited_case: King v. Illinois Cent. R.R., 337 F.3d 550, 556 (5th Cir. 2003)
brief_sentence: The Fifth Circuit has established clear standards for when spoliation sanctions are appropriate, requiring proof that the party acted in bad faith—mere negligence is insufficient. King v. Illinois Cent. R.R., 337 F.3d 550, 556 (5th Cir. 2003); Condrey v. SunTrust Bank of Ga., 431 F.3d 191, 203 (5th Cir. 2005); Guzman v. Jones, 804 F.3d 707, 713 (5th Cir. 2015).
brief_block: The Fifth Circuit has established clear standards for when spoliation sanctions are appropriate, requiring proof that the party acted in bad faith--mere negligence is insufficient. King v. Illinois Cent. R.R., 337 F.3d 550, 556 (5th Cir. 2003); Condrey v. SunTrust Bank of Ga., 431 F.3d 191, 203 (5th Cir. 2005); Guzman v. Jones, 804 F.3d 707, 713 (5th Cir. 2015).
opinion_block: An adverse inference based on the destruction of potential evidence is predicated on the "bad conduct" of the defendant. . . . Therefore, King must show that ICR acted in "bad faith" to establish that it was entitled to an adverse inference.
finding_analysis: King squarely holds that, in the Fifth Circuit, a party seeking an adverse inference based on spoliation must show that the opposing party acted in bad faith; an adverse inference is predicated on 'bad conduct.' The brief's proposition that the Fifth Circuit requires bad faith for spoliation sanctions is directly supported. Although King does not use the exact phrase 'mere negligence is insufficient,' the bad-faith requirement necessarily excludes mere negligence as a basis for sanctions.

proposition: The Supreme Court has long recognized the prejudicial impact of arrest evidence where no conviction exists.
brief_sentence: The Supreme Court has long recognized the prejudicial impact of arrest evidence where no conviction exists. Michelson v. United States, 335 U.S. 469, 475–76 (1948).
brief_block: The Supreme Court has long recognized the prejudicial impact of arrest evidence where no conviction exists. Michelson v. United States, 335 U.S. 469, 475–76 (1948).
opinion_block: Arrest without more does not, in law any more than in reason, impeach the integrity or impair the credibility of a witness. It happens to the innocent as well as the guilty. Only a conviction, therefore, may be inquired about to undermine the trustworthiness of a witness. Arrest without more may nevertheless impair or cloud one's reputation.
finding_analysis: Michelson does recognize the prejudicial impact of arrest evidence, but the brief's pinpoint cite (pp. 475–76) is off. Pages 475–76 discuss the common-law framework for character evidence generally—explaining that the overriding policy of excluding such evidence is that 'its disallowance tends to prevent confusion of issues, unfair surprise and undue prejudice'—without focusing on arrests specifically. The arrest-specific discussion (including Justice Jackson's statement that 'Arrest without more does not, in law any more than in reason, impeach the integrity') appears later in the opinion at page 482. The proposition has support in Michelson overall, but the cited pages do not establish the specific point about arrest evidence.

proposition: Evidence must be excluded under Rule 403 when any minimal probative value is substantially outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice, confusion of the issues, and waste of time.
cited_case: United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979)
retrieved_case: United States v. Stephen Roderick McRae
brief_sentence: Under Rule 403, this evidence must be excluded because any minimal probative value is substantially outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice, confusion of the issues, and waste of time. United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979).
brief_block: Under Rule 403, this evidence must be excluded because any minimal probative value is substantially outweighed by the danger of unfair prejudice, confusion of the issues, and waste of time. United States v. McRae, 593 F.2d 700, 707 (5th Cir. 1979).
opinion_block: Relevant evidence is inherently prejudicial; but it is only unfair prejudice, substantially outweighing probative value, which permits exclusion of relevant matter under Rule 403. Unless trials are to be conducted on scenarios, on unreal facts tailored and sanitized for the occasion, the application of Rule 403 must be cautious and sparing. Its major function is limited to excluding matter of scant or cumulative probative force, dragged in by the heels for the sake of its prejudicial effect.
finding_analysis:
The McRae opinion discusses Rule 403 at page 707, but the framing there is substantially less favorable to exclusion than the brief suggests. McRae emphasizes that Rule 403 "must be cautious and sparing" and that its "major function is limited to excluding matter of scant or cumulative probative force, dragged in by the heels for the sake of its prejudicial effect." The opinion stresses that only unfair prejudice -- "substantially outweighing" probative value -- justifies exclusion of relevant evidence.

In McRae itself, the Fifth Circuit refused to exclude gruesome photographs of the deceased murder victim, holding that the trial court did not abuse its discretion. The court went out of its way to warn against using Rule 403 "to 'even out' the weight of the evidence, to mitigate a crime, or to make a contest where there is little or none." McRae is thus better authority for admitting probative evidence despite prejudicial effect than for excluding it.

While the cited opinion does recite the Rule 403 standard, citing McRae as authority requiring that evidence "must be excluded" understates the case's pro-admission orientation. McRae supports the general legal framework but does not stand for a rule that evidence of marginal probative value must be excluded.

proposition: Bias evidence must demonstrate actual bias affecting the witness's testimony.
brief_sentence: The Supreme Court has addressed the proper scope of bias evidence, emphasizing that such evidence must demonstrate actual bias affecting the witness's testimony. United States v. Abel, 469 U.S. 45, 52 (1984).
brief_block: The Supreme Court has addressed the proper scope of bias evidence, emphasizing that such evidence must demonstrate actual bias affecting the witness's testimony. United States v. Abel, 469 U.S. 45, 52 (1984).
opinion_block: Proof of bias is almost always relevant because the jury, as finder of fact and weigher of credibility, has historically been entitled to assess all evidence which might bear on the accuracy and truth of a witness' testimony. ... A witness' and a party's common membership in an organization, even without proof that the witness or party has personally adopted its tenets, is certainly probative of bias.
finding_analysis: Abel holds the opposite of what the brief suggests. At page 52, the Supreme Court emphasized the broad admissibility of bias evidence—'Proof of bias is almost always relevant'—and specifically held that common membership in an organization 'even without proof that the witness or party has personally adopted its tenets, is certainly probative of bias.' The Court explicitly rejected any requirement that the proponent demonstrate 'actual bias'; it was enough that membership supported an inference of potential bias. The brief inverts this holding by suggesting Abel imposes a heightened 'actual bias' requirement, when Abel in fact stands for broad and permissive admission of bias evidence on a relevance theory under Rules 401 and 403.

proposition: Under the eggshell plaintiff/thin skull doctrine, a defendant takes the plaintiff as he finds him and is fully liable for resulting damage even when a preexisting condition makes the injury more severe.
cited_case: Maurer v. United States, 668 F.2d 98, 99 (2d Cir. 1981)
retrieved_case: Louis A. Maurer v. United States
brief_sentence: It is a settled principle of tort law that when a defendant's wrongful act causes injury, the defendant is fully liable for the resulting damage even though the injured plaintiff had a preexisting condition that made the consequences of the wrongful act more severe than they would have been for a normal victim. The defendant takes the plaintiff as he finds him. Maurer v. United States, 668 F.2d 98, 99 (2d Cir. 1981).
brief_block: It is a settled principle of tort law that when a defendant's wrongful act causes injury, the defendant is fully liable for the resulting damage even though the injured plaintiff had a preexisting condition that made the consequences of the wrongful act more severe than they would have been for a normal victim. The defendant takes the plaintiff as he finds him. Maurer v. United States, 668 F.2d 98, 99 (2d Cir. 1981).
opinion_block: It is a settled principle of tort law that when a defendant's wrongful act causes injury, he is fully liable for the resulting damage even though the injured plaintiff had a preexisting condition that made the consequences of the wrongful act more severe than they would have been for a normal victim. The defendant takes the plaintiff as he finds him.
finding_analysis: The brief's paragraph tracks Maurer almost word-for-word (substituting 'the defendant' for 'he' as the subject of 'is fully liable'). The brief does not place quotation marks around the language, so the trivial rewording is acceptable attribution rather than a misquote. The proposition -- eggshell plaintiff / thin-skull doctrine with full liability for aggravated harm -- is directly stated in Maurer at the cited page. Supported.

proposition: The Fifth Circuit has reaffirmed that defendants are liable for all consequences of their negligence, even when those consequences are more severe due to the plaintiff's pre-existing vulnerabilities.
cited_case: Koch v. United States, 857 F.3d 267, 279 (5th Cir. 2017)
brief_sentence: The Fifth Circuit has consistently applied this doctrine. In Koch v. United States, 857 F.3d 267, 279 (5th Cir. 2017), the court reaffirmed that defendants are liable for all consequences of their negligence, even when those consequences are more severe due to the plaintiff's pre-existing vulnerabilities.
brief_block: The Fifth Circuit has consistently applied this doctrine. In Koch v. United States, 857 F.3d 267, 279 (5th Cir. 2017), the court reaffirmed that defendants are liable for all consequences of their negligence, even when those consequences are more severe due to the plaintiff's pre-existing vulnerabilities.
opinion_block: We see no error in the district court's application of the well-settled principles set forth in Maurer. . . . [T]he Government is fully liable for his resulting harm and disability, even though Koch's preexisting conditions made the consequences of the Government's negligence more severe than they would have been for an ordinary victim.
finding_analysis: Koch squarely reaffirms the thin-skull rule in the Fifth Circuit. The panel quotes Maurer's 'defendant takes the plaintiff as he finds him' language at length, rejects the Government's argument that the rule is limited to latent conditions, and holds the Government 'fully liable' despite Koch's preexisting osteoarthritis and degenerative spinal disease. That directly supports the brief's proposition that defendants remain liable for all consequences of negligence even when pre-existing vulnerabilities amplify the harm. Supported.

proposition: Louisiana follows the rule that a tortfeasor takes his victim as he finds him and is responsible for all natural and probable consequences of his tortious conduct.
quoted_text: ["The tortfeasor takes his victim as he finds him and is responsible for all natural and probable consequences of his tortious conduct."]
brief_sentence: Louisiana courts have likewise embraced this principle. Lasha v. Olin Corp., 625 So. 2d 1002, 1006 (La. 1993) ("The tortfeasor takes his victim as he finds him and is responsible for all natural and probable consequences of his tortious conduct.").

brief_block: Louisiana courts have likewise embraced this principle. Lasha v. Olin Corp., 625 So. 2d 1002, 1006 (La. 1993) ("The tortfeasor takes his victim as he finds him and is responsible for all natural and probable consequences of his tortious conduct.").
opinion_block: It is clear that a defendant takes his victim as he finds him and is responsible for all natural and probable consequences of his tortious conduct.
finding_analysis: Lasha stands for exactly the proposition the brief cites it for -- the Louisiana Supreme Court holds that a defendant takes his victim as he finds him and must compensate for the full extent of the aggravation of a pre-existing condition. However, the brief encloses the passage in quotation marks and attributes it as a direct quote, while substituting 'The tortfeasor' for 'a defendant' (and dropping the 'It is clear that' lead-in). The substantive holding is correctly conveyed, but the quoted language is not verbatim. Reworded.
